title: Hypergroup Symmetry in Relative Quantum Field Theories and Chiral Algebras
abstract: A QFT is said to be relative if it lives at the boundary of a topological QFT in one higher dimension. We develop a general framework for working with noninvertible symmetries of relative theories in two spacetime dimensions, extending several well-known results for absolute QFTs. We emphasize various new features which arise in the relative setting, including the role of topological surfaces of the bulk, and the appearance of hypergroups and certain generalizations of tube algebras known as dome algebras. Our formalism is particularly well-suited for studying rational chiral algebras, where it predicts that finite symmetries are in explicit one-to-one correspondence with conformal embeddings of finite index.   We describe several implications of our framework for absolute theories. First, we explain how to "glue" together symmetries of the left- and right-moving chiral algebras of a 2D CFT to produce topological line defects of the full theory. Second, we derive a precise correspondence between boundary conditions of a 2D CFT and symmetries of its chiral algebra. This correspondence has several structural corollaries: in diagonal rational CFTs, we demonstrate that the topological line defects of the theory act transitively on its boundary conditions, and further that the identity Cardy state has the smallest $g$-function amongst all boundary conditions, including those which only preserve Virasoro symmetry.   We conclude by illustrating our results in a variety of examples. For instance, we show that, if there exists a rational chiral algebra with central charge $c=8$ whose modular tensor category is the Drinfeld center of the Haagerup fusion category, then it must arise as the fixed points of a rank-2 hypergroup acting on the $SU(3)_1\otimes (E_{6})_1$ chiral algebra.
summary: This theoretical paper introduces a comprehensive framework for studying noninvertible symmetries in relative Quantum Field Theories. It develops novel mathematical machinery involving hypergroups and dome algebras to analyze the relationship between bulk topological defects and boundary chiral symmetries. The work provides deep structural insights into how different components of a quantum theory must correspond mathematically.
main_problem: The paper develops a general framework for handling noninvertible symmetries within relative Quantum Field Theories (QFTs) in two spacetime dimensions, extending known results from absolute QFTs.
main_result: It establishes correspondences between finite symmetries in rational chiral algebras and conformal embeddings of finite index, while also detailing how to construct full theories by 'gluing' left- and right-moving chiral symmetries.
method: The methodology involves developing formalism using hypergroups and dome algebras to analyze the bulk-boundary correspondence in TQFTs. It relies heavily on advanced algebraic structures like Modular Tensor Categories.
model_or_system: The focus is on Relative QFTs, which are defined as living at the boundary of a higher-dimensional Topological QFT (TQFT). The mathematical tools involve chiral algebras and their associated representation categories.
key_observables: Topological line defects, boundary conditions of CFTs, hypergroup structure constants, and the $g$-function of boundary states.
important_parameters: The central charge ($c$) of the underlying conformal field theory; the rank of the hypergroup action (e.g., rank-2 for specific examples).
main_assumptions: The framework assumes rationality of chiral algebras and relies on extending known results from absolute QFTs to the relative setting.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate state/operator correspondences ($	ext{Va} \leftrightarrow 	ext{Rep}(V)$), how automorphisms act topologically ($S_g$), and the construction of full theories by compactifying 3D TQFTs on a solid ball.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the general framework for relative symmetries, detailing specific implications for absolute CFTs (like boundary condition correspondences), and concluding with concrete examples involving complex algebraic structures like hypergroups acting on known chiral algebras.
why_interesting: While highly abstract, the deep connections drawn between symmetry groups, topological defects, and underlying algebraic structures (hypergroups) provide powerful mathematical tools applicable to understanding exotic phases of matter or boundary physics in condensed matter systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: The Hilbert space Va consists of boundary local operators O(z) on which the bulk anyon a can terminate. By the state/operator correspondence, it can also be viewed as the Hilbert space that the bulk TQFT assigns to a disk which is punctured at the center by the anyon a.

Figure 2: Left: the representation of an automorphism g of a chiral algebra. Right: the action of an automorphism g on a (non-genuine) local operator O(z) in a module Va produces a local operator (g · O)(z) in Vg∗(a).

Figure 3: The twisted module Va consists of (non-genuine) boundary local operators O(z) which live at the junction of a g-twisted anyon a and the boundary topological line junction Lg. By the state/operator correspondence, Va can also be thought of as the Hilbert space that the bulk TQFT assigns to a disk with boundary condition described by V and with insertions of topological defects as shown on the right.
Figure 4: The bulk to boundary functor FB→∂.
Figure 5: A (non-genuine) local operator O(z) ∈Va can be thought of either as living at the junction of a and Lg as in the left, or at the end of the boundary line FB→∂(a) as on the right.
